title: Lesson 328
transcript: We've talked about forensic investigations and how you conduct an investigation. The last little piece that you need to know about here is that there are, in fact, different types of investigations. Let's start with criminal investigations. Criminal investigations are obviously where you're investigating a crime, the punishment of which is where you might get put in jail. So who conducts, who drives criminal investigations? And the answer very obviously is law enforcement. so here's the tie okay it's very common in an organization for the organization to discover a breach or something that one of their employees have done something wrong and one of When an organization discovers this and realizes, hey, this looks like it's a criminal offense, then very quickly, right away in fact, the organization needs to notify law enforcement and say, we think there's something criminal here. Law enforcement will take a look at this, and if they decide to investigate, it is law enforcement that will drive the investigation, with the support of the organization. The organization can provide log files, things like that, but ultimately who's driving the investigation for a criminal investigation? Law enforcement. Similar with civil investigations. Civil investigations is where it's disputes between different organizations or people. And you may not end up in jail. You'll probably just be fined. But again, with civil investigation, it's still law enforcement that's driving the investigation with the support of the organization. Regulatory investigations are where there's been some sort of regulation that's been violated or breached or something. If there's been a privacy breach, for instance. And who's going to drive a regulatory investigation? The regulator, the privacy commissioner, or whomever is the appropriate regulator. There are industry standards out there by which we can conduct investigations and ISO And ISO 27043 is an example of one of these industry standards that we can use to conduct investigations. The final one that we'll talk about here is what are known as administrative investigations. This is where an organization is conducting their own internal investigation of their own employees or of their own systems. And for organizations, this is by far the most common type of investigation. But remember that tie. If an organization discovers civil or even especially criminal activity that's occurring within their environment, they need to notify law enforcement. Or if they found that they've had a privacy breach, they probably need to notify the regulator. And then if it's criminal or civil, law enforcement's gonna start driving the investigation. And if it's regulatory related, probably the regulator's gonna drive the investigation.
